text: If you are watching this video, you've likely seen me in clinic to discuss the removal of your gallbladder. This is a safe operation, especially if it's done before infection or inflammation sets in. The gallbladder is attached to the underside of the liver. The anatomy of the gallbladder can be very simple, but it requires the dissection of the surrounding tissue to identify the safe anatomy.

Before your operation, you will be given an injection of endocyanin green. This is a dye that illuminates the anatomy of the gallbladder so we can easily identify the structures that we want to cut and the structures we want to preserve. Once we have skeletonized the cystic artery and the cystic duct, we can clip these structures.

A small hole is created in the cystic duct, and a catheter is inserted to flush the ductal system with contrast dye. The dye is visible under fluoroscopy, providing an image of the hepatic duct system to detect any gallstones.

The surgeon places clips on the cystic duct to close the hole made for the cholangiogram, then cuts the remaining cystic duct and artery. Using electrocautery, the gallbladder is detached from its liver attachments. The surgical field is inspected for bleeding before the gallbladder is removed from the body.

The surgeon prepares to remove the gallbladder through an incision to the right of the belly button, using a retrieval bag to extract the organ intact. The incision may be enlarged if the gallbladder contains large or numerous stones.
I hope you found this video helpful.
